english: the 18 regions of hell; tsa-dmyál (tsha dmyal) the hot hell, graṅ-dmyál (grang dmyal) the cold hell. - dmyál-ba-pa (dmyal ba pa), -po (-po), occupant of hell. - *nyál-wa-ćań* (*nyal wa can*) W. poor, miserable, wretched; also like غريب Urd., = my own little self, for 'I', in humble speech.
Cs. to show, dmyúg-dmyúg-pa (dmyug dmyug pa), dmyúg-pa byéd-pa (dmyug pa byed pa) to show repeatedly, to boast. Yet cf. myúg-pa (myug pa).

wound B., C.; ṅá-la rma byuṅ (nga la rma byung) I was wounded; rma byín-pa (rma byin pa) to wound, rma gsó-ba (rma gso ba) to heal a wound; rmai lhá-ba (rma'i lha ba) Sch. 'a wound growing worse'; yet cf. lhá-ba (lha ba). - rmá-k̇a (rma kha) 1. the orifice or edges of a wound. 2. W. inst. of rma (rma) wound, *rúl-li ṭám-te má-k̇a ton* (*rul li tam te ma kha ton*) he has been wounded by the bite of a serpent. - rma-čás (rma chas) Sch. plaster, cataplasm, dressing, bandage. - rma-rjés (rma rjes) Sch. scar, cicatrix. - rma-rnyiṅ (rma rnying) an old wound. - rma-smán (rma sman), rma-rtsís (rma rtsis) medicine or salve for a wound. - rma-mtsan (rma mtshan) scar Bhar. - rma-ró (rma ro) Sch. scurf, scab. - rma-šú (rma shu) a festering, suppurating wound. - rma-šúl (rma shul) scar. - rma-sról (rma srol) Sch. the act of wounding, the wound received (?) - rma-gsál (rma gsal) a fresh wound.
n.p., the river Hoangho Glr.
v. rmá-bya (rma bya).
pf. rmas (rmas) 1. to ask, obs., Lex. 2. to wound Dzl.

(vulgo often *máb-ja* (*mab ja*)), मयूर peacock, living wild in India, an object of superstition with Buddhists and Brahmanists. - rma-bya-čén-po (rma bya chen po) n. of a deity Dom.; rma - čén (rma chen) Wdk., महामायूरी Will: 'one of the 5 tutelar deities of the Buddhists'; Sch.: rma-cen bom-ra (rma can bom ra) 'lord of the yellow stream' (?).
provinc. rmiṅ (rming) Glr. ground, foundation, rmaṅ diṅ-ba (rmang ding ba) to lay a foundation Glr.; rtsig-rmaṅ (rtsig rmang) id.; rmaṅ-rdo (rmang rdo) foundation-stone.
smaṅ-tsér (smang 'tser) or -tsar (-tsar) Sch. 1. pincers to pluck out hairs; Cs. instrument for cleaning the nostrils. - 2. Sch. rake (instrument).
Sch. = rmi-lam (rmi lam), of rare occurrence.
or rather usually: rmád-du byúṅ-ba (rmad du byung ba), rmad-byúṅ (rmad byung) wonderful, marvelous, and ṅo-mtsar-rmád-du gyúr-ba (ngo mtshar rmad du gyur ba) to wonder, to be surprised at, fq.
Sch. wounded; rmás-pa (rmas pa) v. rmá-ba (rma ba).

pf. rmis (rmis), to dream; rmi-lam (rmi lam) resp. mnál-lam (mnal lam) a dream, rmi-lam za-zi (rmi lam za zi) a troubled dream Lt.; mi-bzáṅ-ba (mi bzang ba) a portentous, ill-boding dream S.g.; rmi - lam mtóṅ-ba (rmi lam mthong ba), rmi-ba (rmi ba) to dream, rmi-lam-du rál-bar rmis - so (rmi lam du ral bar rmis so) he dreamt that he had been torn to pieces Dzl.; rmi-lam-du byúṅ-ba (rmi lam du byung ba) to appear in a dream Dzl.; rmi-lam brtág-pa (rmi lam brtag pa) Cs. to judge of dreams, bšád-pa (bshad pa) Cs. to interpret dreams.
Sch. a saddle that may be folded together.

1. hoof, rmíg-pa k̇a-brág (rmig pa kha brag), rmíg-brág (rmig brag) Cs. a cloven hoof, míg-pa-k̇a-brág-čán (mig pa kha brag can) cloven-footed; rmíg-zlúm (rmig zlum) an undivided hoof; rta-rmíg (rta rmig) a horse's hoof, also name of a plant Wdṅ.; gyág-rmíg (gyag rmig) a yak's hoof; rmíg-lčágs (rmig lcags) horse-shoe Cs.; rmíg-(g)zer (rmig (g)zer) horse-shoe nail, hob-nail Cs. - 2. W. horse-shoe, gyab-če (gyab ce) to put on a horse-shoe, to shoe.
lizard, of a small kind S.g.
v. rmaṅ (rmang).
v. dmu (dmu).

Cs. 1. dullness, heaviness. - 2. fog. - rmús-pa (rmus pa) 1. Cs. dull, heavy; Lex. peevish, loath, listless. 2. foggy, gloomy, dark, nam rmús-pa (nam rmus pa) a dark night Dzl., cf. rmúgs-pa (rmugs pa); covered with fog, yul (yul), Dzl. - rmu-tag (rmu thag) 1. a cord to which little flags are attached, on convents etc. 2. Glr. fol. 24, sqq., here the word seems to denote some supernatural means of communication between certain ancient kings and their ancestors dwelling among the gods.
pf. rmugs (rmugs), 1. to bite, B., C., - 2. to hurt, to sting, of bees etc. W.;
